metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the AC1 proving command to anchor to the Model tiering section and assert presence of both harness names, e.g. `grep -A 20 '## Model tiering' SKILL.md | grep -c '^| '` plus `grep -q 'Claude Code' SKILL.md && grep -q 'Codex CLI' SKILL.md`.
evidence: AC1 text: 'checked by grep -n \'Model tiering\' && grep -c \'^\ | \''
The claim requires 'naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier', but grep -c '^| ' is a global row count with no anchor to the Model tiering section and no per-harness assertion.

Verify-command defect taxonomy: (1) substring false-positive — no anchor constraining the pipe-row count to the target section; (5) missing cardinality assertion when criterion names a specific count/content.
findings.finding: AC1's proving command `grep -c '^| '` does not verify the specific claim that BOTH a Claude Code AND a Codex CLI example appear per tier in the Model tiering table. The command counts all pipe-delimited rows anywhere in SKILL.md (including other tables), so it passes even if only one harness is represented or if unrelated tables inflate the count.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: `grep -n 'Model tiering' && grep -c '^| '`
scenarios: SKILL.md already contains other markdown tables; their rows satisfy grep -c '^| ' even if the new tier table omits the Codex CLI column.
A tier table with only one harness column still passes if other table rows exist in the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1 check with a two-part command: (1) `grep -A 20 '## Model tiering' SKILL.md | grep -c '^| '` to count rows within the section, asserting the result equals the expected row count; (2) `grep -q 'Claude Code' SKILL.md && grep -q 'Codex CLI' SKILL.md` to confirm both harnesses are named.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'constraints-in-brief' in the plan (e.g., 'a one- to two-sentence bullet summarising the key constraints passed to the subagent dispatch call') so the executor knows the expected form and the reviewer has an unambiguous standard.
evidence: AC2 text: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default, a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule, and a constraints-in-brief requirement'
'transcription' is defined in the post-AC footnote, but 'constraints-in-brief' has no parallel definition anywhere in the plan.
The Approach section describes what to add to Phase 4d at a high level but does not define what form a 'brief' takes (e.g., a one-sentence bullet? a specific YAML block? a prose paragraph?).
findings.finding: The term 'constraints-in-brief requirement' in AC2 is undefined in the plan. An executor writing Phase 4d cannot determine what prose constitutes a 'brief' for constraints, nor can a reviewer determine whether the written text satisfies this criterion without an agreed definition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'a constraints-in-brief requirement'
scenarios: An executor writes a two-sentence constraints paragraph; a reviewer expects a one-line structured brief — both read AC2 as satisfied.
An automated grep check for 'constraints' in Phase 4d passes regardless of whether the content is a brief or a verbose block.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC2: 'a constraints-in-brief requirement (a ≤2-sentence prose bullet summarising the constraints the orchestrator passes when dispatching the implementer subagent)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the Approach (Phase 4d description) explaining that the constraints-in-brief must reference `.github/module-size-limit.txt` for the module-size bound rather than hardcoding `800`, so AC4 has a corresponding described deliverable in the plan body.
evidence: AC4: 'No hardcoded module-size number is introduced; the brief cites `.github/module-size-limit.txt` (which contains `800`) as the source of truth'
ABSENCE: The words 'module-size', 'module size', '800', and 'module-size-limit.txt' appear nowhere in Context, Approach, or Scope sections.
AC2 describes the constraints-in-brief concept but says nothing about module-size as one of its contents.
findings.finding: AC4 introduces a specific implementation constraint — that the constraints-in-brief must cite `.github/module-size-limit.txt` rather than hardcoding `800` — but the plan body (Context, Approach, Scope) never describes module-size constraints, the module-size-limit file, or why it belongs in the brief. An implementer reading only the plan narrative would not know this file exists or that it must be referenced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
scenarios: An implementer writes the constraints-in-brief (per AC2/Approach) without knowing module size is one of the constraints to include, causing AC4 to fail silently.
A reviewer cannot trace why module-size belongs in the brief — the plan gives no rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section under Phase 4d, add: 'The constraints-in-brief must reference external limit files (e.g., `.github/module-size-limit.txt`) rather than hardcoding numeric bounds, so the brief stays accurate as limits evolve.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (and specify that it appears in 4e-bis) at least two or three concrete observable escalation triggers that distinguish 'model weakness' from normal iteration failure (e.g., repeated structural misreading of the spec, inability to handle multi-file coordination, or a repeated failure class after N clean re-briefs).
evidence: Approach: 'a new ### 4e-bis escalation ladder. Frame tiers as capabilities… The design was pressure-tested across five adversarial review rounds before this change.'
AC3: 'A ### 4e-bis escalation ladder exists that distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers — checked by reading it.'

The plan body gives zero examples of what constitutes a 'model-weakness escalation trigger' vs. a 'normal held-out iteration failure' — there is no enumeration of observable signals (e.g., repeated same error class, failure count threshold, structural misunderstanding of the spec).
T8(a/b): the decision rule (escalate on model weakness) references an undefined trigger vocabulary.
findings.finding: The `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder is described as distinguishing 'normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers,' but neither the plan body nor the Approach enumerates what observable signals constitute 'model weakness.' An executor writing 4e-bis must invent the trigger conditions.
findings.location: Approach section — '4e-bis escalation ladder'; Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: An executor writes 4e-bis with triggers like 'if the implementer fails 3 times' — but this is a quantity invented without authority; a different executor picks 5 times or a qualitative signal.

A harness agent encountering a genuinely under-specified implementation cannot determine whether to escalate (model-weakness) or iterate (normal held-out) — the distinction the plan claims 4e-bis makes is not specified.

findings.finding: The `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder's decision rules (the 'model-weakness escalation triggers') are never enumerated in the plan body — AC3 only says the section must 'distinguish' normal iteration from those triggers, but what signals constitute model-weakness vs. normal held-out failure is entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: '### 4e-bis escalation ladder exists that distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one cited reference (harness documentation URL, changelog entry, or reproducible empirical observation) confirming that Claude Code and/or Codex CLI subagents inherit the orchestrator's model tier when no model is explicitly set, so the plan's problem statement is grounded rather than asserted.
evidence: Plan text: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model, so the subagent silently runs on the strongest tier — the anti-pattern that erases any token saving.'
No citation, documentation link, release note, or empirical test is provided for this harness-behavior claim in the plan text or anywhere in the repo.
The Acceptance Criteria contain no check that validates this harness behavior against external documentation.
The .codex/config.toml in the repo shows environment/shell configuration for Codex but no evidence of model-tier inheritance behavior.
findings.finding: The plan's foundational problem statement rests on an unverified behavioral assertion: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model.' No documentation link, release note, or empirical evidence is cited. If either harness already routes subagents to a cheaper default tier, the problem being solved may be moot or differently shaped — potentially causing the new `## Model tiering` section and Phase 4d standard-tier default to conflict with actual harness behavior.
findings.location: Context / Problem section: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model, so the subagent silently runs on the strongest tier'
scenarios: If Claude Code or Codex CLI already routes subagents to a cheaper default tier, the plan addresses a non-existent problem and the new standard-tier default in Phase 4d could actively downgrade from an already-cost-optimal harness default.
A future maintainer updating the tier table encounters no grounding for the inheritance claim and cannot verify whether it still holds after a harness update.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context/Problem section, add parenthetical citations, e.g.: 'In both Claude Code ([link to Claude Code docs on subagent model selection]) and Codex CLI ([link to Codex CLI docs]) an unset model inherits the orchestrator's model…'. If documentation does not exist, note it as an empirical observation and describe the reproduction steps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing the `## Model tiering` table, verify and cite the Codex CLI mechanism for selecting per-subagent model tiers (e.g., a flag, config key, or API parameter), or explicitly note in the table that Codex CLI tier selection uses a different invocation surface, with a reference to its documentation.
evidence: Plan text (Approach): 'Frame tiers as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'
AC1: 'naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'
The repo's .codex/config.toml contains shell environment and fallback doc configuration for Codex but no model-tier selection mechanism for subagents.
No repo file (AGENTS.md, docs/, examples/) documents a Codex CLI mechanism for launching a subagent at a specified model tier.
The plan text does not cite Codex CLI documentation or a flag/config key for per-subagent model selection.
findings.finding: AC1 requires the `## Model tiering` table to name 'a Codex CLI example per tier', but the plan provides no evidence that Codex CLI exposes a user-facing mechanism to select per-subagent model tiers. If Codex CLI lacks this capability surface, the 'Codex CLI example' entries in the tier table would be inoperative or misleading, making the cross-harness portability claim hollow for Codex users.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1: 'A `## Model tiering` section exists with a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'
scenarios: A practitioner running the skill under a Codex CLI harness follows the 'Codex CLI example' in the tier table, but the referenced mechanism doesn't exist or behaves differently, causing cheap-tier delegation to silently fall back to the orchestrator tier — reproducing the exact anti-pattern the plan aims to fix.

The cross-harness portability argument (the primary justification for capability-tier naming over hardcoded model names) is undermined if one of the two named harnesses cannot actually exercise per-subagent tier selection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalising the `## Model tiering` table, verify that Codex CLI exposes a documented mechanism (flag, config key, or prompt convention) for directing a subagent to a cheaper model tier, and cite the specific surface (e.g. a config-reference key or CLI flag); if no such mechanism exists, replace the 'Codex CLI example' column entry with an explicit 'harness-level: set `model` in codex config' note or mark it as 'harness-dependent / no subagent override supported'.
evidence: Plan text: 'Frame tiers as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'
AC1: 'A ## Model tiering section exists with a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'

scripts/session-shims/codex-config.toml documents Codex CLI's config surface as `shell_environment_policy.set` (static env injection) and hooks with `additionalContext` only — no model-selection mechanism for subagents is mentioned
docs/session-id-shims.md cites the Codex config reference (https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference) for the same surface; no model-tier dispatch surface appears
Evidence class: Unverified — Codex CLI's mechanism for specifying per-subagent model tiers could not be confirmed from the repo or plan text
Prior plan-review cache entry 165daf71f38edae96d02.json flagged the same gap
findings.finding: The plan requires the shipped `## Model tiering` table to name 'a Codex CLI example per tier' (AC1), but the repo's only Codex CLI integration evidence (codex-config.toml, docs/session-id-shims.md, the cited Codex config reference) covers env-var injection via `shell_environment_policy.set` and hook `additionalContext` — not per-subagent model tier selection. No documentation, flag, or config key confirming that a Codex CLI orchestrator can explicitly dispatch a subagent at a cheaper model tier has been located in the repo or cited in the plan.
findings.location: Approach section ('Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings') and AC bullet 1 ('naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier')
scenarios: A practitioner running the skill under a Codex CLI harness follows the 'Codex CLI example' in the tier table, but the prescribed config key or flag does not exist or behaves differently, causing cheap-tier delegation to silently fall back to the orchestrator's model — reproducing the exact anti-pattern the plan aims to fix.
The 'Codex CLI example per tier' entry ships as aspirational text that cannot be acted on, making the cross-harness portability claim hollow for Codex users.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one cited reference (harness documentation, changelog, or empirical observation) in the Context/Problem section confirming that Claude Code and/or Codex CLI subagents inherit the orchestrator's model tier when no model is explicitly set, so the plan's foundational claim is grounded and verifiable.
evidence: Plan text: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model, so the subagent silently runs on the strongest tier — the anti-pattern that erases any token saving.'
No documentation link, changelog entry, or empirical probe is cited for this harness-behavior claim anywhere in the plan.
Evidence class: Unverified — the inherited-model behavior is asserted but not grounded in any observable or cited source
findings.finding: The plan's problem statement asserts 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model' as an established fact, but cites no documentation, release note, or empirical test. This behavioral claim is the justification for the entire `## Model tiering` addition; if either harness already defaults subagents to a cheaper tier, the prescribed 'standard-tier default' in Phase 4d could actively downgrade from a better harness default.
findings.location: Context / Problem section: 'In both Claude Code and Codex CLI an unset model inherits the orchestrator's (expensive) model'
scenarios: If Claude Code already routes subagents to a cheaper default tier (e.g. via a built-in heuristic), the plan's Phase 4d 'standard-tier default' instruction is redundant at best or a downgrade instruction at worst.
A future reviewer updating the tier table encounters conflicting signals between the plan's assertion and actual harness behavior, with no citation to resolve the conflict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define at least one concrete, observable trigger criterion in `### 4e-bis` that signals model weakness vs. a guidance gap (e.g., 'same held-out assertion fails after two full re-delegation cycles with distinct guidance additions'), so an orchestrator can unambiguously decide when to escalate tier rather than re-delegate.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'a new `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder. Frame tiers as *capabilities* …'
AC3: 'A `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder exists that distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers'

Phase 4e (existing SKILL.md): already instructs the orchestrator to re-delegate via 'Describe the missing behavior/contract to the implementation subagent' — creating a competing re-delegation path that 4e-bis must override, but no override signal is defined

ABSENCE: no plan section names observable signals (e.g., repeated identical failure after N re-delegations, a specific error class, a test-coverage pattern) that distinguish model-weakness from guidance-gap
findings.finding: The 4e-bis escalation ladder's trigger criterion is unspecified: the plan says it 'distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers' but never defines what signal tells the orchestrator it is observing model weakness rather than a fixable guidance gap. Without a concrete trigger criterion in the prose, an orchestrator following the skill will have no basis to choose escalation over continued 4e re-delegation, and the ladder will silently never fire.
findings.location: Approach section ('a new `### 4e-bis` escalation ladder') and AC3 ('distinguishes normal held-out iteration … from the model-weakness escalation triggers')
scenarios: Orchestrator re-delegates a failing held-out test to the same cheap-tier subagent 3–5 times per 4e instructions, never recognizing this as a model-weakness signal, and the 4e-bis ladder never triggers.
Two authors interpret 4e-bis differently: one escalates after the first failure, another never escalates. The skill becomes inconsistent across orchestrator instances.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `### 4e-bis` prose, add a trigger sentence such as: 'Escalate the implementer's tier when the same held-out behavioral contract fails across two consecutive re-delegation rounds despite distinct guidance additions — this pattern signals a model capability ceiling, not a missing specification.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move or duplicate the 'transcription' task definition (currently only in the post-AC footnote) into the body of the proposed `## Model tiering` section or directly into the 4d amendment, so the cheap-tier rule and its trigger vocabulary are co-located in the committed SKILL.md text.
evidence: AC2: 'Phase 4d states a standard-tier default, a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule'
Post-AC footnote: 'Transcription in AC2 means a leaf whose happy-path test fully pins the code (1–2 files), so implementing it is mechanical transcription rather than design.'
The word 'transcription' appears nowhere in the Approach body, Phase 4d description, or the proposed `## Model tiering` section spec — only in the AC footnote.
Grep across SKILL.md confirms 'transcription' is absent from the current file; the plan does not specify it will be added to the Model tiering section.
T8(b): a decision rule (cheap-tier applies when task is transcription) is not co-located with the schema/section (4d or Model tiering) where an executor will look for it.
findings.finding: The 'transcription' task class — the sole trigger condition for the cheap-tier rule in AC2/Phase 4d — is defined only in a parenthetical footnote at the bottom of the plan ('Transcription in AC2 means a leaf whose happy-path test fully pins the code (1–2 files)'), not in the proposed `## Model tiering` section or the 4d amendment text. The decision rule and its trigger vocabulary will be non-co-located in the shipped SKILL.md.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 footnote; Approach section — Phase 4d description
scenarios: An executor implementing Phase 4d writes the cheap-tier rule but, not having read the footnote, applies it to any 'simple' task rather than the defined 1–2-file happy-path-pinned class — silently producing a different scope than intended.
A harness agent reading only the `## Model tiering` section and Phase 4d (the natural place to look) finds the rule but not its trigger vocabulary, and must guess the boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `## Model tiering` section, add a prose line such as: 'A *transcription task* is a code-changing leaf whose happy-path test fully pins the implementation (typically 1–2 files, no design judgment required); these are the only tasks eligible for the cheap tier in 4d.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the 4e-bis section (or in the plan's Approach) the terminus of the escalation ladder: either escalate to strong-tier after standard-tier failure, or halt and surface a human escalation signal with the evidence accumulated — whichever is intended.
evidence: Approach: 'a new ### 4e-bis escalation ladder' that handles 'model-weakness escalation triggers'
AC3: the ladder 'distinguishes normal held-out iteration (no escalation) from the model-weakness escalation triggers'

The plan describes only two tiers in the escalation path: cheap → standard (implied by 'standard-tier default' in 4d and cheap as the transcription alternative). It does not specify what happens at or above standard-tier if the standard implementer also fails.
T8(d): fallback for an incomplete/failed sub-step — the sub-step here is the escalated standard-tier implementation; its failure path is unspecified.

The existing SKILL.md Phase 4e has a fallback ('A held-out test fails → describe the missing behavior/contract to the implementation subagent'), but that loop is for a single implementer and does not address multi-tier exhaustion.
findings.finding: The `4e-bis` escalation ladder escalates from cheap-tier to standard-tier on model weakness, but the plan does not specify the fallback when the standard-tier implementer also fails to satisfy the held-out oracle — i.e., whether the ladder continues to strong-tier or terminates with a human handoff. The fallback chain terminus is absent.
findings.location: Approach section — '4e-bis escalation ladder'
scenarios: A standard-tier implementer is launched after cheap-tier escalation; it also fails the held-out oracle after N iterations. The agent has no specified next step — it may loop indefinitely, silently drop to human escalation, or halt with no guidance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (and specify it will appear in the `## Model tiering` section) at least one distinguishing criterion per tier — the task-level property that places work into strong vs. standard vs. cheap — so the dispatch table is a complete decision rule, not just a name-to-model mapping.
evidence: Approach: 'Frame tiers as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap) with Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings'
AC1: 'A ## Model tiering section exists with a capability-tier table naming both a Claude Code and a Codex CLI example per tier'

The plan body never enumerates what task properties qualify work for strong vs. standard vs. cheap tier — no criteria such as 'requires multi-file design judgment' (strong) or 'single-file mechanical transformation' (cheap) are stated.
T8(a): the tier enum vocabulary (strong/standard/cheap) is used as a dispatch key throughout the amendments but its value boundaries are undefined in the plan.
findings.finding: The three capability tier names ('strong / standard / cheap') that will govern all model-dispatch decisions in the amended SKILL.md are introduced in the Approach but their boundary criteria — what distinguishes one tier from another in terms of task characteristics — are never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan specifies only that a table with 'Claude Code and Codex CLI example mappings' will exist, leaving boundary inference entirely to the executor.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Frame tiers as capabilities (strong / standard / cheap)'; Acceptance Criteria — AC1
scenarios: An executor writes the `## Model tiering` section with a table of model examples but no boundary criteria; a harness agent reading it can determine which model to use but not which tasks qualify for which tier — the dispatch decision is left to ad-hoc judgment.
Two different implementors produce incompatible tier tables because the plan gives no criteria, only names.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach, specify that the `## Model tiering` section will include a brief criterion per tier, e.g.: strong = multi-file design with held-out test authoring; standard = implementation against a spec; cheap = mechanical single-file transcription. These need not be exhaustive but must be sufficient to make tier assignment deterministic for the cases the skill addresses.

findings.finding: The Approach names 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d' as explicit deliverables, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that Phases 3 and 4a were actually amended — only Phase 4d is covered (AC2).
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') vs. AC2 (reads only section 4d)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] Phase 3 and Phase 4a each contain at least one model-tier reference (e.g. the word "tier" or "strong-tier") — checked by `awk` or `grep` scoped to those sections in SKILL.md.'

findings.finding: The Approach explicitly describes 'targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d' as deliverables, but Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC5 only verify Phase 4d (AC2), the new Model tiering section (AC1), and 4e-bis (AC3). The Phase 3 and Phase 4a amendments have no covering criterion — an implementer could omit them entirely and still pass all ACs.
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '- [ ] Phase 3 contains model-tier guidance for scoping/planning decisions and Phase 4a contains tier-selection criteria — checked by reading those sections in the updated SKILL.md.'

findings.finding: The Approach names Phase 3 and 4a amendments as load-bearing parts of the model-tiering mechanism (Phase 3 identifies cheap-tier-eligible leaves at dispatch; Phase 4a anchors the orchestrator's own strong-tier for oracle design), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies either amendment. An implementer who satisfies all five ACs could omit or under-specify these two sections, leaving the mechanism incomplete: without a Phase 3 seam note, the orchestrator has no guidance on which leaf types are eligible for cheap-tier dispatch; without a Phase 4a annotation, the orchestrator may not recognize that it must stay on strong-tier during spec derivation.
findings.location: Approach section ('targeted amendments to Phases 3, 4a, 4d') and Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 (or as AC2b): 'Phase 3 dispatch-by-kind names which leaf types are eligible for cheap-tier dispatch — checked by reading the Phase 3 section. Phase 4a carries an explicit strong-tier annotation for spec derivation — checked by reading section 4a.'

findings.finding: The term 'transcription' in AC2 ('a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule') is used without definition anywhere in the plan, leaving an executing agent uncertain about which implementation tasks qualify as 'transcription' versus tasks that do not.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Transcription work means mechanical conversion of a fully-specified stub or pseudocode to the target language — no architectural judgment required. All other implementation tasks default to standard tier.'

findings.finding: The 'cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule' referenced in AC2 is an undefined decision rule: the word 'transcription' (the task class that triggers cheap-tier use) appears nowhere in the plan body, leaving an executor with no vocabulary to implement the rule.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'a cheap-tier-only-for-transcription rule'
findings.suggested_fix: In the `## Model tiering` section, add a row or note defining 'transcription tasks' as a named sub-class of standard-tier work (e.g., 'mechanical tasks with no design judgment: reformatting, boilerplate expansion, literal port of pseudocode to code') and reference this definition in the 4d rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal grep anchor to AC3 and AC6 (e.g., `grep -n '4e-bis' SKILL.md` for AC3 and `grep -n 'cheap-shaped\|strong-tier' SKILL.md` for AC6) so section existence is machine-verifiable alongside the semantic reading check.
evidence: AC3: 'A ### 4e-bis escalation ladder exists … checked by reading it'
AC6: 'Phase 4a carries an explicit annotation … checked by reading Phase 4a'
No grep command is provided to confirm the section header or annotation keyword exists, unlike AC4 and AC5 which supply grep commands.
findings.finding: ACs 3 and 6 are verified solely by 'reading it' / 'reading Phase 4a' with no structural grep anchor to confirm the section or annotation exists. While acceptable for semantic prose checks, the absence of even a section-existence grep means the proving command provides no machine-verifiable evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('checked by reading it') and AC6 ('checked by reading Phase 4a')
scenarios: A reviewer marks the AC green after a cursory read that misses a missing section; there is no failing command to surface the gap.
CI cannot gate on a 'reading' check, so automated verification is impossible.
findings.suggested_fix: AC3: append `— existence check: grep -n '4e-bis' SKILL.md`. AC6: append `— existence check: grep -n 'cheap-shaped' SKILL.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-line maintainer note in the `## Model tiering` section (or the 4d brief) warning that `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is the canonical source and must be updated in sync with any rename, OR add a CI `grep`-based check that the file still exists at the referenced path.
evidence: AC4: 'the brief cites `.github/module-size-limit.txt` (which contains `800`) as the source of truth — checked by grep for the filename in SKILL.md'
No mention of a CI lint rule, a symlink, or even a prose note in SKILL.md warning maintainers that renaming this file requires updating the skill.
T7(b): change introduces a new cross-document dependency; T5e(a): dependency is acknowledged but not mitigated
findings.finding: SKILL.md will contain a prose reference to `.github/module-size-limit.txt` as a living source of truth, but no guard or note in the plan ensures that reference stays valid if the file is ever renamed or removed — creating a latent stale-reference risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 / Scope section
scenarios: A future refactor renames or moves the file (e.g., to `.github/limits/module-size.txt`); SKILL.md's reference silently becomes wrong and downstream agents receive a broken pointer.
A reader following the SKILL.md brief searches for the file and finds nothing, eroding trust in the guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `## Model tiering` section, immediately after citing `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, add: `> **Maintainers:** if this file is renamed, update every `grep`-able reference in SKILL.md to match.` Optionally add a one-liner to the repo's lint/CI script: `test -f .github/module-size-limit.txt || (echo 'module-size-limit.txt missing' && exit 1)`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
